nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving blackwater streams, small creeks, and flooded forest pools in the upper Amazon basin (Rio Nanay and tributaries); heavily shaded, leaf-litter-rich environments with extremely soft, acid, tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: dark substrate (black sand or fine gravel)
dried Indian almond leaves and other leaf litter
driftwood branches and roots for cover
dim lighting with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce) to diffuse light
dense planting with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, Cryptocoryne)
blackwater tannins from peat filtration or alder cones

diet.notes: The Coral Red Pencilfish is a micropredator — an animal that feeds on very small prey — taking tiny invertebrates, zooplankton, small worms, and insect larvae in the wild. In the aquarium it generally accepts high-quality micro-pellets and micro-granules, finely crushed flake, frozen or live baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), micro worms, Daphnia, and Cyclops. Its small mouth limits food particle size to roughly 0.5–1.0 mm, so oversized foods should be avoided. Regular live or frozen foods are important for conditioning and colour development.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras tolerant of acid water (e.g., cardinal tetras, ember tetras)
dwarf corydoras (e.g., Corydoras pygmaeus, C. habrosus)
small Apistogramma species (with caution — provide cover)
freshwater shrimp (Neocaridina/Caridina in a species or biotope setup)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them
boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Nannostomus mortenthaleri; check current FishBase page at https://www.fishbase.se/summary/Nannostomus-mortenthaleri.html for updated image availability.

summary: The Coral Red Pencilfish (Nannostomus mortenthaleri) is a small Peruvian blackwater species described in 2001, with males displaying vivid coral-red and white body coloration. It requires very soft, acidic water and a dimly lit, tannin-rich setup, making it best suited to experienced aquarists.
careNotes: The Coral Red Pencilfish (Nannostomus mortenthaleri, described by Paepke & Arendt in 2001) is one of the most visually striking pencilfish in the genus, but its strict blackwater requirements make it suitable only for experienced aquarists. The species is endemic to the Rio Nanay drainage in Loreto, Peru, where it inhabits extremely soft, acid, tannin-stained waters — parameters that must be replicated in captivity for long-term health and breeding. pH must be maintained between 4.5 and 6.0; anything above pH 6.5 tends to suppress colour and eventually compromise immunity. Total hardness (gH) should be kept below 5 dGH, and many specialist keepers target 1–3 dGH using reverse-osmosis water buffered with peat or supplemented with commercial blackwater extracts and Indian almond leaves. Filtration must be gentle — sponge filters are ideal — as the species inhabits near-still waters and strong current will stress the fish. Lighting should be subdued; floating plants and tannin-stained water both help diffuse light to appropriate levels. A group of 8 or more is recommended; males in good conditions exhibit intense coral-red display coloration and perform rivalry posturing, and a balanced male-to-female ratio reduces inter-male tension. The species can be confused with Nannostomus rubrocaudatus (sometimes called the Ruby Tetra or Purple Pencilfish), which shares red caudal coloration; N. mortenthaleri is distinguished by its more extensive coral-red body patterning and the contrast of its white lateral stripe. Most specimens available in the hobby are wild-caught imports from Peru, though captive-bred fish are increasingly offered by specialist breeders and retailers. Regardless of origin, acclimate new arrivals slowly using a drip acclimation process and quarantine for a minimum of 4 weeks before introduction to a display tank. The quarantine tank should also use soft, acid water — standard pH-neutral quarantine conditions will add unnecessary stress.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is achievable but challenging, and reports are uncommon in the hobby. Condition the group on live or frozen Artemia nauplii and micro worms for 2–4 weeks. A dedicated breeding tank of 20–40 L with RO water, pH 5.0–5.5, gH 1–2 dGH, gentle sponge filtration, dense Java moss or spawning mops, and very dim lighting has been used successfully. Spawning is typical of pencilfish — eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants and not guarded; parents will eat eggs if not removed. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours at 26°C; fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercially prepared first foods before graduating to Artemia nauplii. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced due to the precise water chemistry demands and the tiny size of fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus mortenthaleri
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided full species account including native range (Rio Nanay, Peru), water parameters (pH 4.0–6.0, temperature 23–28°C, hardness 18–90 ppm / 1–5 dGH), adult size (to 3.5 cm), diet (invertebrates/zooplankton), tank setup guidance, and notes on confusion with N. rubrocaudatus.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus mortenthaleri
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes), adult size (max 3.5 cm TL), native range (Peru, Amazon basin), original description citation (Paepke & Arendt 2001), and distribution data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General pencilfish care guide used for trade-level context on group size recommendations, feeding micro foods, tank setup with subdued lighting and leaf litter. Aquarium Co-Op does not have a dedicated N. mortenthaleri page; general pencilfish guidance applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Nannostomus mortenthaleri profile
additional.notes: Species profile used for cross-check of water parameter extremes, coloration description (coral-red display in males), confusion with N. rubrocaudatus, wild collection from Peru blackwater biotopes, and minimum group size guidance.

additional.name: Paepke H.-J. & Arendt K. (2001) — original species description
additional.notes: FishBase reference entry for the original description: Paepke H.-J. and Arendt K. 2001. Neue Nannostomus-Art aus dem peruanischen Amazonasgebiet (Pisces, Characiformes, Lebiasinidae). Mitt. Zool. Mus. Berlin 77(1):3-10. Used to confirm year of description (2001) and authors.

fish.breedingNotes: The Coral Red Pencilfish is an egg scatterer. Successful spawning requires a dedicated breeding tank filled with reverse-osmosis water at pH 5.0–5.5 and gH 1–2 dGH, furnished with fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Parents must be removed after spawning, as they will eat the eggs. Fry are very small and should be started on infusoria before graduating to Artemia nauplii.
